data.title: Energy Transfer: The Operating Angle That Needs Covering
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Energy Transfer (ET) is still one of my biggest holdings right now and I have no intention of changing this, only to increase it. It has continued to perform well and the market seems to finally wake up to the true value of the business, an EBITDA growth machine leveraging energy demand from data centers whilst diverging cash allocations to higher yield areas of the business instead. Its ability to maintain and continue to increase the distributions is solid. Contracts with hyperscalers have meant over 9% of the capacity is secured, a figure I see rising to 20% even this year if the trends keep up. Energy scarcity might be a real thing in the sector, and ET is well positioned to leverage this because of its extensive network of pipelines. I’m still very bullish on the stock and I’m reiterating my Strong Buy rating since November of last year.

Since Last Coverage

The last article I wrote on ET was on November 25. Since then the unit price has provided a nice 10.5% return outperforming the S&P 500 during the same period. During this period this has also been quite a lot of news coming out, some of which I’ll cover today, like the suspension of the Lake Charles LNG project and the issuance of another $3 billion in senior notes. The market seems to have reacted well to the news though given how the unit price has moved.

There was no distribution since my last article, but one just prior to when I wrote the piece. Looking ahead, I expect the next distribution to come in February and to show at least a 3.1% growth rate YoY, similar to what ET increased it last time, so in other words, $0.335.

Above you can see what I expect ET to distribute for 2026, as a base. I do see ET having the ability to go beyond this and I’ll explain more below, but essentially it has to do with diverting investments away from risky projects along with securing long contracts for exports, primarily tied to their LNG segment. Combined though, I see ET distributing $1.3521 per unit, implying a FWD yield of 7.51%. It’s slightly below the analyst estimates of $1.37 but again, my estimates are a base case, there's a strong argument it will be more than that.

The Operating Angle

So in my last coverage the main points I made about why ET was a strong buy (still very much is) was about the consistency across all the segments. The interstate or pipeline segment provides incredibly strong cash flows and reliable revenues because of the location and access into the Permian Basin. I also made the argument that ET is better positioned than many other midstream companies in the AI boom and I think we’ve seen this exact fact play out as it’s contracted to supply 900,000Mcf/d to Oracle (ORCL) for three of its data centers, 2 of them being in Texas.

I also found the valuation to be very attractive and I think that finally, the market is waking up to the true value of the units and the rally we’ve seen since November could very well continue this year as well. It’s further supported by a tighter natural gas market outlook where rising prices has the knock-on effect of inducing higher production, meaning more demand for ETs pipelines. In other words, ET can charge higher fees and drive top line growth.

Scrapping Risky Project, Prioritizing Predictable Cash Flows

Back in December of 2025 ET announced it was suspending the Lake Charles LNG project. It had been ongoing for about 10-years and $350 million had been used to try and reach a final investment decision. The issue I think many had was that ET was trying to sell off a lot of the equity of the project to others, seeing as the price tag would have been north of $10 billion.

The firm had sought to sell 80pc of equity in Lake Charles LNG before reaching a final investment decision (FID), which would have still required ET to inject $3.5bn of capital, based on US Gulf coast projects typically costing around $1,050/t.

But, then on top of this ET would still have to inject a lot of capital, further straining the balance sheet. There were a number of other issues as well, like higher labor costs in the Gulf Coast, which raised the project price tag, but also ET needing to charge higher liquefaction fees, making it a less competitive alternative.

Offtaker interest was never an issue for Lake Charles LNG, which had already secured binding contracts for 11.9mn t/yr, or 72pc, of its nameplate capacity

Demand was never really an issue with over 70% of the capacity being secured. But given how the energy market has shifted over the past few years since the initial project launch I think ET is coming out on the other end of this in a better position. Canceling was the right decision as it frees up capital which is better used in building pipelines and reducing bottlenecks in the Permian as data centers are built around it, and in need of connecting.

In 2026, Energy Transfer expects to invest $5.0 billion to $5.5 billion in growth capital, primarily on projects enhancing its natural gas network.

As per ETs 2026 outlook it sees a decent amount of growth capital being deployed, supporting this exact area I’ve laid out, gas pipelines. The current network of pipelines for ET directly overlaps with some of the the areas where the biggest datacenters will be built, in Texas and around the Permian Basin.

The AI Contract Realization Has Begun

ET has contracted 900,000Mcf/d in supply contracts for Oracles, but then also another 300,000MMBtus/d for Fermi Americas datacenter campus, which same as Oracles datacenters are located in Texas. There are two more worth mentioning as well, the deal with Entergy Louisiana (ELC) for 250,000MMBtus/d via the Tiger Pipeline and 450,000MMBtus/d to Cloudburst Data Centers.

The thing here worth mentioning is that a lot of these contracts are for very long periods, 10 - 20 years in total. These contracts alone bring a combined 1.9 Bcf/d in legally binding supply contracts. The total gathering volume for ET is in the range of 21.6 Bcf/d, which means it managed to secure 9% of its pipeline over the next decade. More deals will likely come out this year too. I honestly don’t think it's unreasonable to see this figure jump to 20% by 2026 year-end. Hyperscalers are looking for secure energy sources. Nuclear has been mentioned a lot, but it takes too long to build. Gas is both the short and long-term solution in my opinion, less capital-intense to build and incredibly reliable as well.

I think the smoking gun here is the figure that this contract means in terms of revenues. In the Q3 presentation 6 Bcf/d in contracting translates to $25 billion in transportation fees, spread out over 18 years. Dissecting this I can assume that 1.9Bcf/d equals $7.9 billion in guaranteed revenues. Because it’s then also spread out over 18 years, it implies an average firm fee of $0.63/Mcf. Using that figure we can estimate what these datacenter supply deals mean in terms of revenues. We take the contract multiplied by the firm fee, and then multiply again by 365 to the annual figure.

The bears might push back and say that $440.3 million in annual revenue additions is nothing for a company doing nearly $80 billion in TTM revenues. But to that I’d say you have to consider the quality of these revenues. They are in the interstate segment, which has a 77.8% EBITDA margin based on the last 9 months ($1.814 billion / $1.413 billion). $440.3 million becomes $339 million in EBITDA. Because ET also trades at a EV/EBITDA of 8.61x on a FWD basis, implies $3 billion more in EV.

Assuming no changes to the cash and debt, the EV would be $140.939 billion, 2.1% above the current figure. These are EBITDA flow through revenues that directly improves the distribution profile for ET. $4.421 billion distributed in the past 12 months. The $339 million essentially adds another 7 - 8% in capacity for growth. Even if ET won't increase it by that, it still has the added effect of improving the distribution profile and lowering the leverage for the company, which in and of itself could lead to a higher unit multiple instead.

Nederland Flexport - Providing A Near Monopoly On Ethane and Propane Exports

The Nederland Flexport is one of the most important assets ET has and one of the most crucial pieces of infrastructure in the US right now. The country has increased both production and exports of oil and gas. ET enables this to happen because of this port.

In the US ET and Enterprise Product Partners (EPD) are effectively running a duopoly of ethane exports with ET controlling the majority but also having more flexibility because of the Nederland port being capable of switching between ethane, propane, butane and ethylene at the same dock.

The NGL & Refined Products reported $18.7 billion in revenues over the past 9 months and $3.065 billion in adjusted EBITDA (16.3% margin). This segment EBITDA represents 25.9% of the consolidated figure.

The strategic expansions are supported by long-term commitments with targeted returns in the mid-teens (sub-6.0x EBITDA build multiples).

In the 2026 outlook from ET they say they target a 6x EBITDA build multiple. The allocated Capex for the NGL and Refined Products segment in 2025 was 27% of the budget, or $1.242 billion. Based on the outlook for 2026, the guidance of $5.25 billion in growth capex (using the mid-range) implies about $1.41 billion towards NGL and Refined Products. With the build multiple it would constitute about $235 million in EBITDA per year.

Again, going back to my estimate that the datacenter contracts could in theory increase the EV by 2.1%, the same logic can be applied here. $235 million in EBITDA becomes $2.023 additional EV using the same multiple as earlier. Combined I’m looking at about 4 - 5% at least in pure EV growth once these expansions are fully operational. Again, this goes back to ET having more flexibility and opportunity to raise the distributions in the future.

The Partnership expects to continue to target a long-term annual distribution growth rate of 3 to 5 percent.

Even with 3 - 5 % target distribution growth, ET still is positioned as a growth opportunity because of the rising EBITDA I see in the near term. Investors might not see most of their investment returns now coming from the distributions, but rather from the unit price increasing because ET is efficiently raising the EBITDA. Over the past 12 months it has reported $14.8 billion in EBITDA. The 2 figures I mentioned for EBITDA growth included $339 million and $235 million. Combined, assuming no other organic or non-organic growth it represents a 3.8% increase YoY. This is more than double the sector median on a FWD basis, which is 1.47%. It makes the argument that ET deserves a higher multiple right now.

Risk

The main risks posed to ET right now I think includes debt and leverage. It announced $3 billion more in senior notes this year. In the last report ET reported $63.1 billion in debt, up from $59.7 billion at 2024 year end. I think we can make the assumption that ET now has north of $65 - $66 billion in debt after this announcement. We’ll know for sure on February 17 when the Q4 report is set to be released. The blended interest was 5.4% on the notes, divided into $1 billion each and the earlier being due in 2031 and the latest in 2056. This means about $162 million more in annual interest expenses.

The company has $3.371 billion in TTM interest expense, this new debt adds 4.2% more per year. It does mean that part of the earlier mentioned figures on higher EBITDA and ability to increase the distributions goes away. The EBITDA generated from the Nederland project for example would see a large portion in theory go towards paying for this new debt. ET is well balanced and the cost of debt is still quite manageable, 5.4% blended is not that high and reflects the quality credit rating it holds. It doesn't take away from the fact that ET is still seeing quite an aggressive rise in debt and if market demand can’t keep up, it will weigh on its ability to raise distributions in the long-term.

The Full Picture

Energy Transfer is still a very high quality midstream company and one of the best in the market right now. The 10% rise since my last coverage I think is the start of a general uptrend for the unit price as the partnership invests aggressively in new pipelines and diverts away from riskier projects like Lake Charles. On top of this, increasing capacity at Nederland means ET has an even firmer grip on the US exports of various commodities like Butane and Propane. It’s ability to maintain or even rapidly raise the distributions I think is very sound right now. Just like last time, I think ET is still a Strong Buy for investors looking for both income and growth, this time argued through the lens of the operating improvements and shifts ET has been able to do in a short amount of time.

data.title: PTA: Discount Widening Makes This CEF Appealing Again
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund (PTA) is a closed-end fund that has seen its discount widen materially since our prior write-up in October 2025. CEF share prices can trade wildly different from their underlying net asset value per share based on supply and demand principles.

This is unlike exchange-traded funds or traditional mutual funds. ETFs have creation/redemption mechanisms to help keep them tethered to their underlying NAV price; traditional mutual funds are bought and sold at the end of the day at NAV. With that can come a unique opportunity, but also potential risk for CEFs, making them a relatively unique asset wrapper.

PTA Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.91
  • Discount/Premium: -6.77%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.23%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.68%
  • Leverage: 34.22%
  • Managed Assets: $1.77 billion
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation of October 27th, 2032)

PTA's investment objective is quite simple: "high current income." They also have a secondary objective that is similarly simple: "capital appreciation."

To achieve this, they will invest "at least 80% of its managed assets in a portfolio of preferred and other income securities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. companies, which may be either exchange-traded or available over-the-counter." They will also "seek to achieve favorable after-tax returns for its shareholders by seeking to minimize the U.S. federal income tax consequences on income generated by the Fund."

Like nearly all the (CNS) closed-end funds, the fund operates with leverage, and that leverage comes with additional costs. That takes the fund's total expense ratio up to 2.86%. However, like the leveraged CNS CEFs, the fund was largely hedged with interest rate swaps. That kept the fund from truly experiencing the peaks of the higher rate environment.

On the other hand, the variable-rate financing costs are still higher than the fixed-rate financing costs. Therefore, unless we get some aggressive rate cuts, the fund is still likely to experience some higher financing rates.

Performance - Discount Widens Pressures Returns But Adds Appeal

Since our last update, the fund has provided some positive total returns. Relative to the S&P 500 Index, for some color, it has significantly underperformed.

However, as a preferred/fixed-income fund, it is really added only for some color. Instead, performance compared to a blended benchmark for this fund that is provided can be more appropriate. The index is based on the ICE BofA 7% Constrained DRD Eligible Preferred Securities Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The indexes have no expenses to contend with, but they are also non-leveraged, which has given PTA a boost in terms of total returns. At least, on the NAV total return calculations, whereas the market price total returns have slipped in the last quarter—and that's precisely what we are focusing on today.

While the fund's discount has widened significantly since our prior update, it technically bounced off of our exact 'Buy' discount level of 8%. However, at nearly a 7% discount , we are still trading at a wider discount compared to its long-term average—and close enough to our discount level that I'd be comfortable adding here.

Some pressures that could be facing a fund like PTA are expectations for higher potential long-term rates. While the Fed has been in cut mode for their benchmark rate, which would help to ease the borrowing costs that PTA could face after the interest rate swaps fall off, the fund is still sensitive to long rates as well. With greater uncertainty in the current U.S. administration, geopolitical tensions, and an ever-growing deficit, there could be some real pressure on risk-free U.S. Treasury Yields that keep them elevated or head higher.

For now, we've seen mostly stabilization in the 4-4.5% range, which has boded well for the fund in the last few years after the initial jump in both short- and long-term rates. This discount has opened up precisely because the NAV has held steady, or even technically has been trending higher since last April. At the same time, the share price has been what has been under pressure. Thus, the resulting widening discount we're noting here is adding appeal to consider an investment in PTA.

Distribution - Steady Monthly Pay

Since the fund's inception, the payout has been incredibly steady; the only adjustment was a small increase at the beginning of 2023. That was even despite the pressure from short- and long-term rates.

At the current price, the distribution yield comes in at 8.23%, but thanks to a sizeable discount, the fund's NAV rate comes to 7.60%. The NAV rate is important because this is what the underlying portfolio has to earn in order to have the payout covered.

Being a preferred/fixed-income fund, we would like to see higher coverage come from net investment income. NII is simply the interest and dividends the fund collects (total investment income) minus the fund's expenses. In the last fiscal year, we've seen a sizeable increase in the NII for the fund. In fact, the highest the fund has delivered since its inception.

That helped to increase NII coverage to over 71%; however, that still means the fund needs to produce some capital gains in order to achieve coverage of the payout. At this point, with the NAV relatively flat over the last few years, that suggests they've been able to achieve that. NAV is still down from the $25 inception NAV due to the rate pressures in 2022 and 2023.

For tax purposes, the fund has seen primarily ordinary income characterizations.

The good news is that this has largely been qualified dividends, making it tax-advantaged, as its name would suggest. That makes it more appropriate for a taxable account.

For the fiscal year ended October 31, 2025, for individual taxpayers, the Fund designates $76,056,531 as qualified dividend income eligible for reduced tax rates, $148,882 as qualified business income eligible for the 20% deduction and interest related dividends of $9,863,344. In addition, for corporate taxpayers, 51.38% of the ordinary dividends paid qualified for the dividends received deduction (DRD).

PTA's Portfolio

Previously, we had noted that the fund shifted its strategy slightly, where it had previously limited itself to holding only 20% or less in contingent convertible bonds (aka "CoCos" or AT1 bonds). At that time, we didn't get the reporting that provided whether they were moving in that direction further or not.

With the latest annual report, we can see that they have taken the allocation fairly materially to 43.8% of net assets or 28.9% of managed assets.

Contingent Capital security (CoCo). CoCos are debt or preferred securities with loss absorption characteristics built into the terms of the security for the benefit of the issuer. Aggregate holdings amounted to $512,409,047 which represents 43.8% of the net assets of the Fund (28.9% of the managed assets of the Fund).

That was up from the 35% and 22.6%, respectively, at the end of April 30, 2025. This can be worth watching because these types of securities can be riskier relative to other preferred and fixed-income offerings, as they are the first to absorb losses when things go sideways.

On the other hand, they are often issued by larger financial institutions that should otherwise operate with pretty solid balance sheets. Where we saw the major risk play out was when Credit Suisse was taken under by UBS Group (UBS).

With that, it is important to consider that the fund is fairly solidly split between higher-quality and lower-quality names at about 50/50.

The reason for the significant weighting toward investment-grade is that most of the fund's underlying portfolio is invested in well-known, large financial institutions, G-SIBs (global systemically important banks). These are names that are under heavier regulations and government oversight. Worth pointing out is also the fund's weighting to each position is rather low, which provides significant diversification, too.

In total, they listed 264 total holdings—meaning that no single issuer is likely to see significant pressure play out on the fund, but more of an overall market downturn or event would have to occur. Something as we saw with rates heading higher, pressuring the fund and its average modified duration that comes in at 8.3 years, making it incredibly interest rate sensitive.

This is a result of holding a significant portion of its portfolio in higher-quality investment-grade ratings but also holding a heavy allocation to perpetual preferred instruments. These are perpetual non-cumulative preferred offerings so that they count toward Additional Tier 1 Capital for banks.

Conclusion

PTA has seen its discount widen significantly, and that could be due to uncertainty going forward. It also could have been impacted by the fund seeing its discount narrow quite materially, while the anticipated liquidation date is still years away. With that, it has once again become an appealing choice for investors looking for a place to put some cash to work while collecting some solid distributions. NII coverage also improved quite materially in the latest fiscal year, which bodes well for the distribution to remain where it is.

data.title: Intel: Advanced Processors To Drive Momentum In 2026 And Beyond
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Intel Corporation (INTC), driven by the Core Ultra Series 3 processors, which are made from Intel’s 18A (1.8 nanometer wafers), the company’s most advanced semiconductor. The 18A semiconductor is integrated with FinFET and RibbonFET, which improve its electrostatic control and enable fast switching and better scaling. The Core Ultra Series 3 processors under the 18A semiconductor have the capability of delivering around 15% improved performance, 30% better chip density compared to its 3-process node. This high-level performance is required by advancing AI computing, which requires small, densely packed transistors for better performance.

Core Ultra Series will power over 200 different PC designs, including mobile phone processors, and I think this presents a huge market reach in the AI PC market, which keeps shifting to scaled performance. For example, the AI PC business is estimated to contribute to a number of benefits, such as around 213% Return on Investment (ROI). The company plans to kick off pre-orders on January 27th, 2026, and they will be available globally, which indicates that Arizona Fab 52 has the capacity for scheduled volume production of 18A. So far, Arizona Fab 52 is bigger than the TSMC Arizona Fab and produces more than 40,000 wafers per month. This is more than double that of TSMC, which is currently producing around 20,000 wafers per month.

Also, looking at it with an external picture, I believe Core Ultra Series is likely to drive 2026 momentum, looking at the AI Pc processor market, which is rapidly growing at a CAGR of 42%. I also believe that the current Intel’s one-year price return of 153.75%, beating the S&P 500 at 18.55%, is a reflection of this optimism.

I will now move to a deep dive below, unearthing why I think Core Ultra Series processors will likely drive a bullish momentum for Intel.

Company Brief

Intel manufactures and markets computing products globally. The company operates through Foundry, Intel products, and other segments such as CPUs, GPUs, SoCs, FPGAs, memory networking, AI optimization, autonomous driving solutions, edge platforms, digital transformation tools, and advanced packaging. The company was incorporated in 1968 and is headquartered in Santa Clara.

Advanced Processors Driven Growth

Basing my opinion on AI PCs, the rise of AI is also progressing the need for AI PCs, as personal computers continue driving the demand for AI PC processors. The demand for CPUs with NPUs such as Intel Core Ultra Processor Meteor Lake, Arrow Lake, and Lunar Lake for AI performance is increasing. The demand is driven by the AI infrastructure, such as new data centers and chip manufacturing, which is increasing the supply of AI utilities such as AI PCs.

The impact of AI is estimated to contribute around $1 trillion to GDP and influence around $4.4 trillion in consumer goods purchases in the US alone. This signals that the usefulness of AI is taking shape. So far, it is estimated that AI is capable of automating around 18,000 tasks and 1,000 jobs in the US labour market. The value of this work, which is estimated to reach a market value of $4.5 trillion in the US, can be handled using AI, which is a sign that the return on AI is imminent.

This is why I think AI PC solutions and benefits are likely to result in a 213% ROI, 90% reduction in hardware-related visits, and 65% reduction in device time spent on management. I believe this will increase the demand for AI PC, and this is why the market for AI PC is likely to grow rapidly at a CAGR of 42%. In 2026, the market volume might hit around $25 billion and reach $34.6 billion by 2029.

Another way I am looking at the AI PC market as a major tailwind for Intel Core Series processors is that the AI PC percentage share of the total PC market shows an impressive increasing trend. After a 15.6% in 2024, followed by 31% in 2025, the market share of AI PC in 2026 is estimated to hit 54.7%, indicating that AI PCs are outpacing other older PCs. This is hinged on performance, such as performance speed, better battery performance, which the advanced processors provide.

From a centralized look, I think this external tailwind is in favour of the Intel Core Ultra Series because it is powering more than 200 PC designs. This indicates how Intel Core Series is ready for a diverse market uptake. In other words, Core Ultra Series could also be the most widely adopted AI PC platform that Intel has ever launched, which is why I believe it presents Intel’s momentum. For example, the Panther Lake, which is already in production but volume production is scheduled later in 2026, is on track to meet customer commitments. In my view, given that this is the first Intel 18A wafer that is scalable for an AI PC performance, it is broadly essential in the commercial and consumer AI PCs, edge solutions, and gaming devices. This is why the market share of AI PC in 2026 is expected to hit 54.7%, up from 31.0% in 2025.

In terms of PCs, Core Ultra Series is likely to strengthen Intel’s market leadership in PCs. For example, in gaming performance, a comparison between Intel Core Ultra X9 388H with Intel Arc B graphics delivering 45W is better compared to the NVIDIA 4050 laptop GPU, which provides 60W. Also, the average frames per second (FPS) indicates that Intel is delivering more or slightly better performance with an average 10% faster overall and lower sustained power compared to NVIDIA’s PCs. This shows that Core Ultra Series is improving Intel’s competitiveness against NVIDIA mainstream gaming laptops, which signals its competitiveness.

Comparative Analysis

I will compare Intel with QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), some of its peers, on several fronts. Beginning with revenues, Intel’s revenues have been plummeting from 2021 to 2025. This decline has been driven by weakening PC demand from the pandemic surge, intense competition from AMD and Nvidia, and delayed advanced chip manufacturing. Nevertheless, despite the weak momentum, its revenue has remained higher than its peers, an indication of its broad revenue base and market dominance. QCOM, whose revenues are $44.28 billion, still lags behind Intel's, which are at $52.9 billion, a slight down by 0.47% YoY. The same case applies with AMD looking at its $32.03 billion, meaning Intel is still dominating the processor market.

Intel’s 23.9% EBITDA margin shows an uptrend from 2024 to the current 2025 gain, and it’s the second-best among the three companies. Its uptrend is a sign of its margin recovery and improving profitability. The fundamentals discussed above are coming out well, as EBITDA reflects recovery after a constrained period between 2021 and 2023.

Intel has been grappling with a deepening financial crisis due to a huge investment in its foundry business. Now, EBITDA reflects operational efficiency and a high possibility of profitability. This is much clearer in FY2025's net loss, which reached -$267.00 million, significantly down from -$18.756 billion in FY2024. The foundry business has not yet recorded a major turnaround, but the projected volume production of 18A at Arizona Fab 52 shows potential hinged on the Core Ultra Series,18A wafers. In my view, looking at Intel’s EBITDA compared to its peers’ EBITDA trend, Intel has been volatile, driven by the foundry business, while its peers' EBITDA has not been volatile. In my opinion, the volume production from the company’s Arizona foundry is likely to significantly turn around EBITDA margin, positioning the company into profitability.

The foundry is expected to begin volume production of the Core Ultra Series in 2026. If this happens successfully, I expect it to sustain Intel’s current profitability momentum and restore top-line growth.

Moving to earnings estimates, Intel has just released its fourth quarter 2025 earnings, and it shows a start of an impressive future EPS by beating EPS estimates by 0.71%. I believe consensus estimates for FY2026 at $0.49 are possible after beating its 2025 estimates, given the current profitability trajectory. This indicates that the company is gearing up its profitability momentum and has a solid execution capability for Panther Lake CPUs, which are offering superior performance by 77% better gaming speed and AI capabilities.

Remember, 18A is already under high-volume production, which says that the scheduled global availability of Core Ultra Series is likely to materialize early this year as planned. The processors are now at the disposal of over 200 designs from varied leading global partners in the AI PCs. This gives a sign of a major inflection point for Intel foundry services, which has kicked off strongly with production of 18A.

Valuation

I will value Intel by comparing its P/S multiple with that of its peers, AMD and QCOM. Intel has a P/S multiple of 3.86x compared to AMD’s 13.16x and QCOM’s 3.86x.

In my view, this tie with QCOM indicates Intel’s current undervaluation. AMD’s P/S ratio is around 3 times higher than Intel’s P/S ratio. With Intel’s forward revenues expected to reach $53.78 billion in FY2026, up by 1.76% YoY, this is the highest volume among its peers, signaling its market dominance. Compared to AMD, with an estimated forward revenue of $6.57 billion representing 65.39%, and QCOM's estimated forward revenue of $45.96 billion, up by 1.59% YoY growth, it follows that INTC's revenue growth is far lower than AMD. However, with the expected 2026 positive turn of events in the foundry business, I expect the company’s revenue to grow beyond 2026, which aligns with the consensus estimates. As such, I expect a market rerating to a P/S of about 5x-10x.

Investment Risk: Execution Risk

I think the major investment risk right now is execution risk around the Core Ultra Series 3 global rollout. While this is the most advanced and first platform built on Intel 18A, powering over 200 PC designs, any delay in the Core Ultra Series global rollout is a major potential risk that could materially reduce its revenue projections. For example, from the Q4’2025 earnings deck, which shows a revenue of $4.5 billion from the foundry business, it still posted a $2.5 billion operating loss, which underscores the structural challenge in scaling advanced nodes such as 18A. There is a potential mismatch between product optimism and financial strain, which represents a possible execution risk. Therefore, I am urging investors also to keep an eye on the global rollout of 18A and the progress of volume production.

Conclusion

I believe Intel is at a major inflection point led by the 18A manufacturing capability, which is also driving its market leadership in PC CPUs such as the Core Ultra Series. This is likely to drive its revenues, which is why I am optimistic by reiterating my bullish rating.

data.title: Nebius: Ignore The Volatility - Follow The Capacity
data.description:
Nebius (NBIS) has given a ~30% cheaper opportunity than the highs we saw in October 2025. For a stock that has gone through a breathless couple of quarters before that, hardly allowing an entry for those (including me) who believed in the fundamentals but found the rerating too fast to react to, corrections create a window where new entries can be calmly evaluated.

We are now at September 2025 levels, not really a huge fall that makes fresh capital deployment easy, but definitely easier than the one-way traffic we saw even from September to the October highs. My analysis finds great potential in the demand; that's a given. Economic progress is also very supportive. But valuations are still not easy to ride, despite corrections. The valuation pressures are very visible given how Nebius corrected despite a stellar Q3.

The stock needs more than perfection in execution to rerate or force revisions that can support upsides from here on - at least in the short term. My analysis suggests Nebius is still ripe for fresh investments. This is purely driven by how the fundamental strength looks extremely good and expectations from execution that can absorb some more valuation compression while still delivering some upside. The markets have not been very kind to narratives over the past quarter, but Nebius' narrative looks strong enough and is backed by execution so far that it increases the odds of further short-term and long-term gains, even in the current selective market environment.

Stellar Growth Supports the Rally

Nebius' 2025 rally is steeped in consistent high growth, visible even in 2024, but confirmed at scale in 2025. Q3 2025 revenues were around $146m, still up quite strongly YOY and even sequentially (almost 40% up QoQ). The high double-digit growth rate was slightly down by the standards of the past 2-3 quarters and even missed expectations. This is mostly attributable to supply readiness. The demand story strength remains unaltered with capacity sold out (and revenue recognition therefore merely shifted, not fully lost). However, high momentum and high valuation stocks are allowed to react to supply chain or capacity timing issues, so some valuation compression is justified.

However, given the stage of growth Nebius is in (for context, we are talking about high double-digit growth numbers on a sequential basis), a topline miss of the magnitude seen in Q3 (less than 10% below estimates at ~$158m) is minor at best. More so, when looked at from the lens of ramping up revenues due to some capacity coming online during the quarter and its contribution not fully reflected for the total Q3 revenue numbers (but should mean an already higher exit run rate number). The Israel data center facility came online fully during Q3. The first tranche of the New Jersey expansion was handed over to Microsoft. This is not likely a material number on the exit run rate but counts (not material because Microsoft's revenue contribution in 2025 is already framed as immaterial). Existing site capacities were also reported as being scaled, and revenue ramp due to this should have increased through the quarter.

Revenue growth is expected to bounce back again in Q4, with analyst expectations pegged at ~$246m, i.e., another blockbuster sequential rise of ~70%. Management guidance indicated landing somewhere around the midpoint of the $500-550m mark for the full year. Taking out reported revenues for the first 3 quarters of the year, that translates to around $220m as revenues in Q4, still a ~50% growth sequentially. Analyst estimates peg the expectations towards the higher end of the revenue guidance range.

Finally, the long-term revenue visibility appears dependable with a ~$4b pipeline generated in Q3 (up ~70% sequentially). The near-term demand for 2026 is also well funded. The Microsoft deal ($17.4-$19.4b) should ramp up significantly in 2026 and reach full run rate by 2027. The Meta deal should also see material ramp-up this year. Nebius has confirmed that half the $7-9b ARR expected in 2026 is already booked.

Economics and Capital Discipline Good

While it is too early to look at operating leverage at a stage where Nebius is going through infrastructure buildout, margin improvements are already underway. Core infrastructure EBITDA margins expanded to 19%, improving sequentially. Group EBITDA is expected to be positive for Q4.

Another factor to keep an eye on during this buildout phase, funding and capital discipline, looks disciplined. Capex (~$5b) is aggressive and likely to remain so if demand is to be met, so dilution risks do exist and should be closely watched. So far, financing remains diversified - a combination of asset-backed debt, corporate debt, and ATM equity. GPU spending accounts for ~80% of capex and is generally triggered by visible demand. Debt is generally secured against assets and cash flows tied to signed hyperscaler contracts. Overall, spending appears to be controlled with an eye on dilution. It does not raise an alarm at this stage.

Valuations and Embedded Risks

Accounting for ~$3.5b in revenue in 2026 as per analyst expectations in Seeking Alpha, the EV to forward revenue ratio is actually higher than the sub-7x levels seen around the April 2025 lows. The chart below shows rerating up to ~60x at some point because they were based on the higher end of the guided revenue spectrum for 2025. With Q2 and Q3 providing further visibility on 2026 ARR and most of it already booked, the updated current-year revenue expectations mean valuations have been earned through a strong spurt in revenue growth in 2025 and sealing the supply-demand mechanics for 2026 to a large extent.

The only risks that need to be ridden now do not directly include a narrative-based rerating, in my view. We need to only assess the execution risks pending to achieve expected revenues in 2026 and whether supply will come online on time. I think the path to profitability is already ahead of expectations, so as long as that story does not deteriorate materially, it should not impact sentiments negatively.

Demand and Supply Dynamics

The ARR expectations of ~$7-9b in 2026 are half funded already. Hyperscaler contracts are multi-year, back-end loaded, but reliable. So the ARR to revenue realization rate in 2026 becomes the dominant story to track. The expected ~$3.5b revenues translate to ~45% of the midpoint of the ARR expectations. At a similar stage last year, Nebius was looking at an $875m exit ARR for 2026, mostly back-end loaded. The revenue expectations were $600m at the midpoint of a wider range. Revenue expectations have been reset (capacity timing effects as discussed) to slightly lower at $525m (midpoint guidance), but exit ARR guidance for the year is now expected to be $1b at the midpoint. The net effect is that ARR to revenue realization for the year at the start of the period was ~68% and is converging toward ~53% now. These numbers are based on wide ranges in expectations but are indicative of what kind of revenue realization is realistic. The ~45% revenue realization for 2026 is therefore certainly plausible, even accounting for scale effects. Particularly given that through most of 2025, supply constraints were a known theme and hyperscaler contract effects were relatively subdued than what is expected in 2026.

Another way to contextualize how realistic the revenue trajectory is to look at the expected sequential average growth rate required. For the revenue to grow from ~$246m in Q4 to ~$3.5b for the whole year, Nebius' topline needs to nearly double sequentially on average (~95% sequential growth on average) until Q4 2026. That is the average requirement every quarter, not literal - the pace of ramp (guided to be back-end loaded) will clearly be lumpy and take off at some point, not necessarily in Q1 or even Q2.

That's a tall ask considering Nebius' average sequential growth of ~70% in 2025. This is a risk fresh investments will ride for most of 2026 and is, in other words, the same downstream form of the risk that the rest of the unbooked ARR poses. In fact, while the revenue realization affects the valuation multiples, it is ARR risks that should ideally be tracked more importantly. The revenue growth risks are directly tied to how well the remaining ARR commitments show up. And the activation speed will show up in revenue end states. There is a good chance that the ARR exits strongly, even higher than guidance estimates (like what happened in 2025), while revenues do not meet expectations due to delayed ramp-ups.

A revenue miss could pressure valuations in 2026, but the greater pressure will come from an ARR deceleration. I feel the remaining ARR risk for 2026 (beyond what is now booked) looks less speculative than levels that warrant caution simply because the demand signals remain very robust and the pipeline generation remains strong too ($4b in Q3 alone and growing sequentially in high double digits). At worst, I see Nebius turning to utilization at less conducive economics to fill capacity if the bookings do not stay strong and conversions from an impressive pipeline are lackluster. However, as of today, this is highlighting tail risks as the worst-case outcome, and that is far from the base case expectations in terms of meeting (or even exceeding) exit ARR targets for 2026.

Overall, ARR activation risks exist but appear well covered. The supply risks, which were the gating variable in 2025, should be a telling factor in 2026, determining how much of the ARR goals are realized, as well as the timing of the revenue recognition. The supply roadmap has better visibility today, with several supplies already coming live in Q3 itself. The Israel, UK, Finland, and New Jersey expansion is already online and mostly pre-booked (with some capacity close to roll-out and should be in effect in Q4). That ramp-up is going to be augmented strongly in 2026.

That's why we say that in 2026, we will be securing 2.5 gigawatt total contracted capacity. And we are planning to physically build 800 to 1 gigawatt of connected data centers. This will be done by the end of next year.

The connected power buildout is conservatively lower than the contracted power, showing Nebius' conservative buildout approach with an eye on matching incremental demand with supply coming online. The capex spends are also balanced, with only land, power, and infrastructure spends committed earlier (less than 20% of overall costs).

How to Play Nebius Now

Nebius' 2026 journey will focus on progress on connected power milestones. Plus, the ARR trajectory should remain on track, with the demand and supply side still looking strong. Other things I will keep an eye on include hyperscaler deployment updates and capex discipline as buildout continues.

Nebius' story is so fast-paced that we often talk in terms of sequential growth and progress. So, every quarterly earnings session becomes important, particularly in deciding the short-term impact on share prices. I expect revenues in the $220-250m range, consistent with management’s full-year midpoint guidance and late Q3 capacity coming online. More important than headline revenue is a higher exit run-rate and a clear rebound in incremental ARR, which was constrained in Q3. Margins should at least hold near ~19%. I will closely track connected capacity milestones, hyperscaler deployment progress, ARR commentary, and capex discipline. A revenue timing miss is tolerable if ARR momentum and supply visibility remain intact. ARR deceleration should require a revision of my Buy thesis.

Although the short-term dynamics and steep expectations may lead to volatility in the near term, my Buy call on Nebius is comforted by the long-term story anyway. Even if there are near-term misses and timing issues, the route to long-term revenue realization could still be intact. I have reconstructed a slower hypothetical path over the next few years to counter the consensus estimates (as per Seeking Alpha estimates). The hypothetical path shows that even a material miss in 2026 still retains a high probability path to the same long-term valuation math - as long as ARR activation is delayed and not lost. And conservatively, the analyst revenue curve already assumes sharp multiple compression beyond 2026, with EV/Revenue falling toward ~1x by 2029-30. The alternative path may see slightly elevated valuation in 2026 (or see short-term corrections), but does not need to do a lot of heavy lifting in the later years to compensate for any immediate slower-than-expected progress.

The long-term thesis needs to remain strong, though, which is how I see it today. Although competition in AI infrastructure will increase, I think indications today show that the market itself is growing much faster than any single provider can supply. As enterprise and sovereign AI use increases, demand should stay strong and structural (not cyclical). Backed by secular demand, Nebius only needs to keep building and activating capacity in a disciplined way - the need to win outright only impacts short-term dynamics.

data.title: DOL Vs. VEA: Different Approaches Result In Similar Results
data.description:
Introduction

Always on the lookout for that “better mousetrap,” even within my long-term-held “Core” ETFs, I recently compared two International ETFs where the younger ETF, the Avantis All International Markets Equity ETF (AVNM), was outperforming the better-known Vanguard Total International Stock Index Fund ETF Shares (VXUS). This led me to do the same analysis against the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF Shares (VEA) by comparing it to a newer competitor, the WisdomTree True Developed International Fund ETF (DOL).

If the choice is between these two International Development market ETFs, take the lower-cost option: VEA, but both are rated Hold due to U.S. stocks having better results over the past decade.

Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as (edited):

The fund employs an indexing investment approach designed to track the performance of the FTSE Developed All Cap ex U.S. Index, a market-capitalization-weighted index that is made up of approximately 3,957 common stocks of large-, mid-, and small-cap companies located in Canada and the major markets of Europe and the Pacific region. The Advisor attempts to replicate the target index by investing all, or substantially all, of its assets in the stocks that make up the index, holding each stock in approximately the same proportion as its weighting in the index. VEA started on August 17, 1999. Benchmark: FTSE Global All Cap ex US TR USD.

This ETF has $204B in AUM and comes with only 3 basis points [bps] in fees. The TTM yield is 3.1%.

Index review

A good due diligence starting point for any index-based ETF is with the index the portfolio is tied to. Here, the index manager describes their index as:

The FTSE Developed All Cap ex US Index is part of a range of indices designed to help US investors benchmark their international investments. The index comprises large, mid, and small-cap stocks from developed markets excluding the US. The index is derived from the FTSE Global Equity Index Series (GEIS), which covers 98% of the world’s investable market capitalization. The Index has almost 3900 stocks spread across 24 countries.

Source: research.ftserussell index,

Why the benchmark against the much larger (47 countries, 8500 stocks) is a mystery and of little sense.

Holdings review

Starting with region allocations, Europe and the Pacific comprise 87+% of the portfolio.

Drilling down to countries reveals the top two to be Japan and the UK; nine countries have a weight of over 5%.

Note that, unlike MSCI, FTSE considers South Korea to be Developed, not Emerging. When looking at sectors, without the large U.S. technology stocks, the Tech sector ranks only fourth in allocation, with Financials moving to the top.

Even with the large number of holdings, the Top 20 still comprise almost 17% of the total weight.

At the bottom, there are over 2600 positions, each with a weight of at/under .01%, which in total comes to only 10% of the portfolio.

Distributions review

While payments are quarterly, the June/December will tend to be bigger as some international companies only distribute semi-annually at those times.

WisdomTree True Developed International Fund ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as (edited):

The investment seeks to track the price and yield performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree International LargeCap Dividend Index. The fund normally invests at least 95% of its total assets (exclusive of collateral held from securities lending) in constituent securities of the index and investments that have economic characteristics that are substantially identical to the economic characteristics of such constituent securities. The index is a fundamentally weighted index that is comprised of the large-capitalization segment of the dividend-paying market in the industrialized world outside the U.S. and Canada. The fund is non-diversified. DOL started on June 16, 2006. Benchmark: MSCI EAFE NR USD.

This ETF has $740MM in AUM with a higher fee of 48 bps. The TTM yield is 2.7%.

Index review

While DOL benchmarks against the MSCI Index, it invests based on an in-house one. With each ETF based on a different index, that increases the need to understand both indices used.

The WisdomTree True Developed International Index* is a fundamentally weighted index that measures the performance of the large-capitalization segment of the dividend-paying market in the industrialized world outside the U.S. and Canada, including South Korea, Poland, and Taiwan. The Index is comprised of the 300 largest companies ranked by market capitalization from the WisdomTree International Equity Index and dividend-paying companies in South Korea, Poland, and Taiwan, whose market capitalization exceeds the smallest company selected from the WisdomTree International Equity Index. Companies are weighted in the Index based on annual cash dividends paid. The Index was established with a base value of 200 on May 31, 2006.

Source: WisdomTree index.

For more on this index, use this link.

Holdings review

WisdomTree did not provide regional data; thus, it started with country-level allocations.

The top two are the same between the ETFs, but the Japanese weight differs by 4%.

The top sectors match with Technology moving up one position here.

With only 300 holdings, DOL is much more concentrated than VEA. Here, the Top 20 stocks are 28% of the total weight.

The bottom third all come in with weights of .01% or less, comprising 10% of the portfolio weight.

Distributions review

The same pattern of payments as VEA shows appears here.

Comparing ETFs

Starting with the Vanguard Style Boxes, we can see that DOL only holds Large-Cap stocks, whereas VEA has 21% of its allocation below that market-cap.

DOL is also more value-oriented than VEA, with a 10% shift from Growth into Value allocation. When comparing sector allocations, the differences are small in my view.

There are several major country differences. VEA has Canada exposure; DOL has Taiwan; the other does not. As mentioned before, VEA has a larger exposure to Japanese stocks.

Risk and returns

Using PortfolioVisualizer, we find the following.

VEA has a slightly better CAGR; DOL has less risk. When comparing Sharpe and Sortino ratios, those results balance each other out, resulting in similar ratio values. The difference in market-cap and Value/Growth allocations, while generating the same results so far, does provide skilled investors with the opportunity to pick the ETF they believe should be the better performer in today's market climate.

If the choice is between these two International Development market ETFs, take the lower-cost option, VEA, but both are rated Hold due to US stocks having better results over the past decade.

Portfolio strategy

For USD investors, the constant question is whether adding International stocks adds value to their portfolio. Since that reversed over the past year, some allocation might make sense.

One benefit is that International Development market stocks have shown a .72 correlation factor to U.S. stocks, which should then smooth out a portfolio's annual performance results compared to having no international exposure.

Asset class: U.S. Equity

Objective: Seeks long-term capital appreciation

Portfolio management

William Scott Priebe

Portfolio Manager

Fund tenure since 2009

José Muñoz, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Fund tenure since 2017

Market Environment

2025 was one of the most speculative markets Geneva has participated in. The riskiest corners of the equity market, particularly anything tied (directly or loosely) to AI, played an outsized role in driving index-level returns. While that trend is understandable, what has made this year so unusual is the persistent “two-track” narrative: headlines have been dominated by tariffs, politics, and a clear cooling in parts of the labor market, yet most major asset classes delivered strong results and risk pricing remained remarkably calm. By year-end, that resilience was visible in both prices and spreads.

The S&P 500® Index ended 2025 near record highs with a 16.4% advance, delivering its seventh double-digit increase over the past nine years. That said, a large portion of these gains were again concentrated around large-cap technology companies with the equal-weighted S&P only increasing 9.3%. After April, large-cap earnings estimates continued to drift higher, but it seems that small-cap earnings are finally starting to broaden and catch-up. Credit remains relatively calm as well with investment-grade option-adjusted spreads sitting at 0.8%, and high-yield spreads at 3.0% in December, both near the tighter end of their post-2008 ranges and a clear signal that markets are still rewarding risk-taking rather than paying investors to worry. In this juxtaposed backdrop, investors may reasonably ask: what kept the rally alive since the April lows, and what could change as we turn the page into 2026?

We believe the answer is tied to liquidity, easing financial conditions, and sustained enthusiasm for growth narratives which are all now reinforced by a Federal Reserve that is actively easing as labor conditions soften. Especially within Geneva’s small- and mid-cap benchmarks, Index performance was largely driven by multiple expansion, not earnings growth, which supports the idea that the market has been excited about lower quality, momentum-based companies. The Russell 2000 Growth Index experienced over 20% earnings multiple expansion while our small-cap portfolio saw 9% multiple contraction despite delivering stronger fundamental earnings results.

Performance Review

Investing in high-quality companies was a headwind to performance this quarter; within the broader US equity universe, low quality companies (those rated B or worse) returned 4.52% versus 2.11% for high quality (those rated B+ or better). Headwinds in the Russell 2000 Growth Index were much the same; nonearning companies returned 7.1%, companies with a market cap of <$250M returned 12.3% and companies with a share price of between $5 and $10 returned 8.5%.

Portfolio Positioning

The Fund continues to be overweight high-quality companies; this includes companies with high degrees of recurring revenue, revenue visibility and consistency. At the factor level this includes an underweight to beta and residual volatility and an overweight to earnings quality and growth risk factors. Outlook

2025’s market “engine” has been AI capital expenditures, easing policy and improved margins, but investors have become increasingly selective about which AI exposure they are willing to reward. The infrastructure buildout remains enormous, but scrutiny is rising. The market’s reaction function has sporadically started to shift from “any AI is good AI” toward questions of ROI, competitive intensity, and second-derivative demand (a dynamic we have seen in prior tech cycles). Even within semiconductors, recent price action has reflected that higher bar – strong results can lift the complex (as Micron reminded markets in mid-December), but leadership is no longer as effortless as it was earlier in 2025. One lingering concern remains market concentration. Even with periodic “broadening” bursts, the large-cap indices are still heavily influenced by a narrow cohort of mega caps, and we see increased concentration even in the small- and mid-cap growth benchmarks.

Various industry and asset-manager research continue to flag historically elevated concentration, lower quality, and speculative stocks outperforming, which raises the stakes for investors who are only playing the index-level outcome. The key question for 2026 is whether earnings growth broadens enough to support the market beyond a narrow set of winners. In 2025, the market has been rewarding duration and narrative, but the fundamental debate is shifting from “how big is the buildout?” to “who captures the economic surplus?” That transition is exactly where we think 2026 becomes more constructive for quality-oriented active management. We are forecasting that the S&P 500 ends 2026 with another positive year but likely a bit more muted than the recent strong double-digits returns we’ve seen. Dispersion is rising and “stock picking” is returning. Goldman Sachs has pointed out unusually low implied correlation among S&P 500 constituents (i.e., individual stocks moving less like one trade), which historically creates a richer opportunity set for active managers. AI is likely to become a “winners vs. losers” adoption story.

Many are anticipating a shift in 2026 from pure infrastructure enablers toward firms that can deploy AI into measurable productivity and margin outcomes, exactly the setup where business quality, balance sheet strength, and execution matter. Putting it together, we are mindful that the path of least resistance remains to the upside for markets, especially with the continued enthusiasm around AI and retail investors piling into speculative areas of the market. That said, we are increasingly optimistic that 2026 should be a better environment for quality active managers with broadening earnings growth, higher dispersion, a more demanding market for fundamentals, and an AI narrative that could migrate from capex excitement toward operational results (where strong companies tend to separate from weak ones). If the market does trend towards quality with small-cap earnings catching up to the large caps, that should bode well for high-quality active managers based on the last 20 years of history. We have lived through several of these more speculative markets before and while each situation is different, the market will eventually correct and focus on company fundamentals, creating the conditions for quality-oriented managers to once again outperform.

Definitions: Average annual total returns are the annual compounded returns that would have produced the cumulative total return if fund performance had been constant during the given period. P/E ratio is the price of a stock divided by trailing 12-month earnings per share. Sharpe ratio measures excess return per unit of risk (standard deviation). A higher Sharpe ratio suggests better risk-adjusted performance. Standard deviation measures performance fluctuation, may not be indicative of future risk and is not a predictor of returns. Alpha represents the excess returns of a fund relative to its benchmark. A positive alpha is the added value an active manager has contributed over the benchmark returns. Beta measures volatility in relation to the fund's benchmark. A beta of less than 1.0 indicates lower volatility, while a beta of more than 1.0 indicates higher volatility relative to the benchmark.
