data.title: Namib Minerals: Separating Restart Hype From Core Value
data.description:
When I first covered Namib Minerals (NAMM), the share price was around $7.87; since then, it has been plummeting hard. At the time, my main thesis was that investors should be cautious. Due to the SPAC structure and overvaluation, the stock seemed to be detached from reality, and the company’s balance was not ideal: it lacked cash for their ambitious plans. Since my first thesis, the company now shows some progress with the Redwing project restart. Also renewed technical and feasibility studies. In the short term, this news positively affected investor sentiment and made some short-term fluctuations in share price.

However, we need to separate technical movement from value creation. Currently, neither the Redwing nor Mazowe projects have a final financing structure or FID. So, these changes just increase optionality and potential upside, but at the moment, this still does not change the company's financial profile.

The Market Got It Right

The first, and I believe the most important, thing is that the SPAC premium is gone. After the listing on the exchange, the market was prepared to pay a premium for an ambitious story. But almost 99% of SPAC investors walking out showed that even at the start, institutional support for this structure did not exist. The market, to be fair, just failed to recognize this fast. Furthermore, I believe that financing risk today is priced in. Although technical activity and project movement are now ramping up, the company’s balance and financing position changed minimally since my first thesis.

The expectations have been lowered. The current capitalization (see my SOTP analysis below) is more reflective of a single mine rather than an entire portfolio of projects. This is an important structural change, as after SPAC it was seen as a growth story, but today it is a combination of limited performance plus uncertain optionality. I do not believe that the market is wrong. It just went from speculations to realism.

What Did Not Change

Even though the share price since the first thesis dropped significantly, the main business problems still remain. The main problem is the company depends on one asset.

How Mine still remains the only real cash flow-generating asset. Annual production is around 24-25 thousand ounces of gold, and their AISC is around $2700-2800/oz. Still quite high AISC in my opinion. At current gold prices, this lets them generate a positive margin, but in absolute numbers, this is a limited-scale operation. Even if gold remains this high, How Mine is not capable of becoming that "cash engine," to independently finance restart projects.

This is where the second problem arises. Mazowe and Redwing projects. We see some action in these projects, as the feasibility study is moving on and preparation activity can be seen; both projects are in the pre-financing stage. The main question remains: where are they going to get the capital? I believe that without clear partnerships or project financing, these assets will mean either new debt or new share issuance, which will result in dilution. Dilution, however, is the main risk in any junior gold miner or microcap investment.

Third, the risk of corporate structure. If we look at annual G&A costs, it is around $20 million. For a company of this size, it is a significant sum compared to its asset scale. In other words, what I want to say is that the structure is too heavy for the current asset portfolio. We see that the market is already punishing this company hard, and fundamentally the business did not become stronger. How Mine has some sort of stability, but it is not creating growth. Restart projects are still depending on external capital. Corporate burn is still putting pressure on shareholders. Don't get me wrong, this is not a bankruptcy situation, but not growth either.

What Has To Change?

I cannot rate this stock as a sell or a buy. Mainly because this is an asymmetric situation, where everything depends on a few catalysts. For the stock to start working, I believe these things have to happen:

  1. First, How Mine needs to maintain its stability without operational delays, which could allow the company to finance their operations. The main risk also comes from this, because if this mine experiences disruptions, we might see some sharp drops.
  2. Secondly, at least one restart project (either Mazowe or Redwing) needs to start moving forward with a clear financing structure (hopefully not harmful to investors). They can use partnerships or project financing, which should drive the share price up if it happens.

Due to these reasons, sell carries opportunity cost, but rating it a buy is not easy either, because the catalyst scenarios depend not only on luck but also on discipline. Furthermore, if there is no clear and shareholder-friendly financing structure in 12-18 months, I think that the risk of dilution will increase significantly.

Valuation

Core

I have done a preliminary SOTP valuation on the company's assets. Let's look first at the conservative and current situation as of January 2026. For this valuation, I am using a conservative method, using only one currently working and cash flow-generating asset - How Mine. This lets us see the true value of the company today, and not through future scenarios that are not yet financed. In my model, I use the assumption that the gold price stays the same as today at $5000/oz, production will be 25k ounces, and AISC will remain high, but it will let the company generate positive cash flow. How Mine discounted NPV is around $285 million. Due to the fact that this is the only working asset, I am not using a risk discount for it.

From this value I am taking out corporate costs, which discounted current value is around $141.3 million, and also net debt of $3.2 million. We get the company's asset value of around $171.1 million, or $2.60 per share. This core valuation explains why the market is still looking at the company with caution, even at high gold prices.

Blue Sky

I am calling this a blue sky scenario because currently we can only wish for it. I have tried to model How Mine with huge Redwing and Mazowe potential. I am using the same gold price as in the valuation above and modeling annual production of 380,000 ounces. To make the model more realistic, I have found what it usually takes for the companies to achieve this type of result and added dilution to the model. If we expect that the company is going to need capital to launch these projects, we should model at least $350 million. This means that the company will most likely need to issue new shares. I have used 170.3 million shares for this valuation. NAV per share is then around $39.84. This type of modeling lets us avoid the "paper value" illusion.

The question is whether the model shows a realistic situation. Not really; it gives us guidelines to evaluate recent news and price fluctuations. This is just a maximum potential vision. The market price, as always, is showing us skepticism, mainly due to financing and jurisdiction. For this scenario to become reality, a few things need to happen: successful institutional capital attraction, political stability in Zimbabwe (they want to tax more due to gold reaching $5000/oz), and disciplined project development. Don't count on this model as a fair value giver. As I mentioned, it just guides what we could see if total success was reached. Many investors got trapped in this stock because they were pricing in the perfect scenario immediately after SPAC.

P.S. Due to fluctuations in share price in recent days, the upside can be drastically changed by the time you read this. Also, Zimbabwe is imposing 10% royalties if the gold price stays above $5000, which might hurt NPV. I will redo calculations on my next update; we will let metal and stock prices settle.

Summary

Namib Minerals has left post-SPAC optimism and transitioned into a phase where the market is valuing operations, not promises. My conservative SOTP valuation, which takes only How Mine assets, shows that the company's value is still standing on high costs and heavy corporate structure, even at good metal prices. The latest announcements about the Redwing restart are fueling market emotions. However, today, when the stock rose 60%, I saw many buy orders that were crushed by those who were taking profits, so be aware of the situation that buying the stock that ran up that fast in a few hours can result in losing even 30% in one day. The company remains dependent on financing and the success of its projects. Due to this reason, I am rating it a buy for traders and a hold for investors. There are junior miner stocks that pose a little less risk currently and have more potential.

data.title: High Yield Vs. Dividend Growth: Why 2026 Is The Year You Can Have Both
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

There is a frequent discussion amongst investors about which is "better" – high yield or dividend growth. Young investors are often lured by the idea of buying a company, holding it for 20 years, and the dividend growing every single year.

It is a very appealing picture that has a few practical problems. For starters, predicting is hard, especially about the future. It is difficult enough to predict that a company is still going to be in business in 20 years and that it won't get bought, go private, merge, or even go bankrupt. Many things can change over 20 years.

The second problem is that dividend growth takes a long time. A 3% yield growing at an annual rate of 10% will take 14 years of dividend growth to reach a 10% yield. So you have a choice: you can invest at 3% today, and maybe you'll have a 10% yield on your capital in a decade or two. Or you can invest in holdings that have a 10% yield right now, and you'll get 10% next year.

It is true that investments with high yields do tend to pay out most of their earnings. As a result, high-yield investments will sometimes reduce their dividends as conditions change. If a company is paying out 100% of earnings, and earnings decline, then they will have to cut their distribution. In the case of RICs (Regulated Investment Companies), companies are required by tax law to distribute the majority of their taxable income. As a result, they can't maintain a large cushion between earnings and their payout, and dividends should be cut when earnings decline to avoid dilution.

The flip side of that reality is that investments that tend to pay out a high portion of their earnings will increase their distributions when their earnings climb. Today, we are going to look at two companies that have cut their dividends in the past when their earnings went through a downcycle. In 2026, we expect both of these companies to see higher earnings, and that will lead to dividend raises.

Pick #1: BRSP—Yield 10.7%

BrightSpire Capital, Inc. (BRSP) is a mortgage REIT that owns mortgages secured by commercial real estate. Over the past two years, many commercial real estate owners have struggled to pay their mortgages. As a result, BRSP has spent the past few years foreclosing on properties.

Since June 2023, BRSP has foreclosed on eight properties with a combined total of $241 million in net carrying value: Source

One of the benefits of the mortgage REIT structure is that when they take over a property, they can actively manage it. Unlike banks, which generally look to dump the asset at any price, an mREIT can operate the property for years if necessary to achieve a beneficial exit price.

When the property is foreclosed on, it is written down to "fair value," which is usually going to be quite low for a property that failed to pay its mortgage. The mREIT then has a variety of options to improve the value, ranging from leasing it up, renovating it, or simply waiting for the market cycle to turn and investor interest in the property to increase. They can sell it quickly to extract capital or wait for the next market peak to sell at a premium price. It requires a constant analysis of opportunity costs. Is the REIT better off selling the property and freeing up capital to make new loans? Or is it better off being patient and possibly selling the property for more in the future?

The bottom line is that, due to pulling in its horns to foreclose on properties and manage other distressed loans, BRSP wasn't originating many loans. Its portfolio as of Q3 2025 was $2.4 billion, when it was over $3.9 billion in Q3 2022. A smaller portfolio produces fewer earnings.

On the Q3 2025 earnings call, CEO Mazzei noted that they are targeting a $3.5 billion portfolio in 2026:

"Our timetable for sale of REO assets will generate liquidity for future loan originations and drive the loan book growth toward our targeted portfolio of approximately $3.5 billion."

To achieve that target, management expects to need to originate about $300 million/quarter because other loans are expected to be repaid. We know BRSP hit that goal in Q4 because they reached it before they announced Q3 earnings:

When BRSP reports its Q4 earnings, we will be looking to see if they are on track to start out the year and if management still thinks it can hit that $3.5 billion mark.

If management can achieve that goal in 2026, there is a high probability that we will see a dividend raise late in 2026 or early 2027 as BRSP ramps up its leverage. Sometimes it really is as simple as a larger portfolio that generates more cash.

Pick #2: EPR—Yield 6.3%

Last year, I didn't take a vacation. At least not a vacation in the sense of traveling to do fun things. Life happened, and time slipped away. This year, some vacations are being planned. But don't worry, my work travels with me!

In this case, I think it is vital to do some due diligence on some properties owned by EPR Properties (EPR). One thing I have never tried is skydiving, and EPR owns several iFLY Indoor Skydiving locations. About 25 years ago, give or take, I fell in love with Pagosa Springs. It is definitely a place worth going back to a time or ten, and The Springs Resort & Spa is owned by EPR.

EPR Properties is a REIT that owns places that are simply fun to go to. Whether you want to hit balls at a TopGolf (which fortunately requires zero golfing skills), do some bouldering at a climbing gym in Chicago, explore the arches of Moab, enjoy a day at the waterpark, or simply enjoy dinner and a movie, EPR is there.

EPR Properties was originally founded as a movie theater REIT. It owned properties that were leased to AMC—which, despite the turmoil in the industry, from a landlord perspective, theaters have been good to EPR. Occupancy has routinely been +99%, and outside the disruption of COVID, rent has been paid on time. The back rent that was deferred during COVID was all paid back. EPR's focus on high-quality locations means that even when Cineworld (branded as Regal in the U.S.) filed bankruptcy, they had fewer properties closed than other REITs that had a fraction of its exposure to Regal.

However, it is a reality that movie theaters aren't as popular as they once were. The model is moving to fewer theaters that offer premium offerings, as opposed to theaters that relied on high volumes. For several years, EPR has slowly reduced its exposure to theaters.

Theaters are still the largest exposure at 37% of annualized adjusted EBITDA, but their largest tenant, Callaway Golf (CALY), is in the "Eat & Play" segment. EPR was the first REIT to start investing in the development of TopGolf locations when it was a start-up company, and the relationship has continued to grow. Source

EPR isn't rushing to dump theaters; it is diversifying primarily through investing elsewhere. We expect that their diversification will accelerate in 2026, as management has laid out a plan to invest $400-$500 million in 2026. Since COVID, EPR has not meaningfully grown its portfolio. Initially, it was simply recovering from the impacts of COVID, and then interest rates were sky-high, and management didn't believe their cost of capital was appropriate for aggressive expansion.

As we go into 2026, EPR has the opportunity to invest at low prices (higher cap rates) thanks to high interest rates, and it has a strong balance sheet to leverage. Source

EPR has room to grow its dividend, and we expect a small dividend raise in 2026. Better yet, we believe that AFFO will be able to grow at a pace closer to 5%, which will drive more significant dividend growth in the future as well as potential for price upside.

EPR is currently trading at a low valuation, about 10x forward AFFO. This is a REIT that is capable of trading at a valuation of 16x AFFO, which would be around $85.

We see a lot to love about buying EPR at current valuations. It's ready to grow after a few years of not doing much. That means higher valuations, higher share prices, and a growing dividend.

Plus, the benefit of that really cool feeling while you're sitting at an EPR property and thinking to yourself, "This place is paying me dividends!" Maybe that isn't the best reason to buy a stock, but it is one of the best feelings in the world.

Conclusion

Do you want to invest in dividend growth or high yield? Why not both? BRSP shrank its portfolio as commercial real estate defaults were piling up. A smaller portfolio led to lower earnings. Late last year, BRSP started ramping up its originations, and we expect BRSP to grow its portfolio by nearly $1 billion in 2026. That will drive higher earnings and lead to the dividend returning to growth.

EPR is a REIT that was hit really hard by COVID and suspended its dividend to protect its balance sheet. Perhaps it isn't a popular move, but it was definitely the smart and responsible move. Following the pandemic, management took a cautious approach to expansion as high interest rates led to an unattractive cost of capital. With interest rates coming down, EPR has signaled that they expect to expand aggressively in 2026. Thanks to their prudent balance sheet management, EPR has ample room to expand responsibly.

As investors, we can get a very healthy yield starting out, and we can expect to see dividend growth in the near future. The best of both worlds!

data.title: Synopsys: Catalysts Are Still Far Away, Wait For A Better Price
data.description:
Investment thesis

Synopsys, Inc. (SNPS) is a software company that its product is used by chip manufacturers to design chips. This industry has been dominated by three players: Synopsys, Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), and Siemens EDA. Synopsys and Cadence stock prices have usually traded at par. As you see in Figure 1, both stocks trade with a gap. That could be an opportunity to buy Synopsys until it gets to the level of Cadence.

I love this business. I am an investor in Synopsys and Cadence. I think there are some catalysts that could raise Synopsys stock price. Its IP business has deteriorated, and I think the company has the levers to improve it and regain its leadership. In the EDA software, Ansys integration can give synergies and capture more value from both solutions integrated. And new AI agentic capabilities can raise even more the value of the solutions as they can replace even more engineers.

However, the value of Synopsys in my estimation is $478.5 per share, down $7.3 per share from its current stock price. Risks are moderate and dependent on external factors and could affect the complete industry. I recommend holding and waiting for a further downturn in the stock because the catalysts won’t appear this year.

The IP business has been a growth driver for the company, and the problems that it has faced, which have hammered the stock price, are an opportunity.

Synopsys’ IP business decreased 8% in 2025 vs. 2024, to around $155 million, with total revenue at $1.7 billion. Operating margin decreased even more, 43% reaching $419 million of adjusted operating income due to the fixed costs of employees developing the solutions. Those results are highly impacted by the US restrictions on China, among others.

Management has acknowledged the problems, especially those that relate to its mismanagement, such as wrong resource allocation. That sincerity gives me confidence in a turnaround. In the long term, I am confident that China’s restrictions will fade away as the problems are provoked by a one-of-a-kind president in a democratic regime. Another leader will have a more diplomatic way of negotiation. Synopsys is a leader in the IP business, being almost twice that of Cadence. Cadence didn’t alert over those problems, and what has stated is that “strong momentum, driven by global accelerating IP demand”. I consider that the IP business, greater in Synopsys, made a much greater impact than EDA software. And Synopsys IP business, more related to the foundry business, was more impacted by regulatory restrictions. Whatever, my impression is that other factors, like mismanagement, have beaten down its revenue and margins in the IP business rather than restrictions in China. However, I consider that Synopsys will be able to catch up with Cadence and reestablish its IP business.

I am also confident that the new royalty model in custom IP will boost Ansys’ multiples. The company is trying to change from NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) + Use Fee to add a royalty that will increase revenue, as the company would get a royalty for each piece sold. ARM Holdings (ARM), which has a similar model, trades at 67.41 vs 34.86 in the case of Synopsys, Figure 3. Anyway, success in this transformation is not assured, and the impact will take time as you recognize revenue of the fee right away, but royalties are cashed 2-3 years later when the product is launched to the market.

EDA: two driving forces

I believe the EDA software business has a lot of fuel to increase its pricing power and contribute to solid revenue growth in the coming years. The integration with Ansys and the agentic capabilities that will change the way the software is used and the value created.

I believe that the original Synopsys EDA software and Ansys software create more value when integrated than as two separate pieces of software. Nowadays, a designer loses time because they first design the semiconductor logic, Synopsys, and then use another software, Ansys, to check the heat, for example. However, if the heat doesn’t fit, then it has to change the whole logic design. This loop could take weeks. With the integration, the designer just has to make a complete design of the semiconductor. The EDA value is based on time-to-market instead of the software. Besides, the integration will eliminate any blind spots due to friction in the transfer of the design from one software to another.

The other driving force is AI's agentic new capability. Basically, the agentic software would do the work the engineers are currently doing. If a work employs 10 engineers with a cost of $5 million, Synopsis could claim could claim $2 million if the software saves half of the engineers. Just to grasp how much revenue could be lifted, if the price could increase 20%-30% for 30% of the customers in three years, it could increase revenue growth by 2-3 percentage points. I am based on a 15-20% increase in bookings and revenue in DSO.ai. The value for Synopsys’ customers is high, especially in the current context of a shortage of engineers. Making the engineers’ work easier, the cost of engineers will decrease, as well as productivity. As AI agentic software would be more intensive, it is an engineer who could work 24 hours, the hardware’s revenue will increase too, otherwise the cloud cost would be high.

Valuation

In my valuation methodology, I discount projected free cash flows at a convenient WACC. I define cash margin as net income adjusted for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax, offering a clearer view of operational cash generation than standard margin metrics (Figure 4).

For 2026, I foresee $9.594 billion influenced by the acquisition of Anys and in line with analysts’ estimates. Later on, I estimate a 10% growth rate in 2027, which will increase to 15% in my ten-year model, higher than the long-term management guidance of 12-13%. This steady increase is supported by a transformation in the IP business and a new model based on royalties. In EDA software, it is supported by getting more value with an integrated solution with Ansys and the development of a new model with AI agentic, as I have stated.

Cash Margin will grow very slightly from 34% to 36% in the ten-year period. For those growth drivers, we have talked about the company must invest in research and development even more than the historical 35%. However, I see improvements due to the integration of Ansys. Net working capital and CAPEX investment will be in line with the historical value. Especially, net working capital changes are difficult to predict.

I estimate Synopsys value at $478.5 per share, down 7.3% compared to the current stock price (Figure 5). The Price-to-Free Cash Flow is 67.8, which is even higher compared to historical values (Figure 6). The company follows the rule of 40 as a good software company, but the free cash flow yield in 5 years is 3%, down from the risk-free rate of 4%.

Figure 6

Risks

The main risk for Synopsys is new restrictions in China. In the current volatile political context, I consider a 65% probability of occurrence. As we have shown, the IP business has decreased by 8% in revenue and 43% in adjusted operating income. Maybe a new restriction won’t damage as much as the previous one, as the company will be more protected, and the easiest business has already left. I could see clearly $200 million a year in free cash flow impact. Here, what is important is that in the former restriction, Chinese start-ups have emerged, and it is currently a threat because those start-ups will keep developing solutions up-market.

There is an exogenous risk that depends on the hyperscalers’ investment. If there is a deceleration in investment, there will be less chip investment in companies like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD),…, which means fewer designs and less revenue for Synopsis and Cadence across the industry. Hyperscaler CAPEX is expected to reach $602 billion, a +36% increase year over year. I don’t expect this movement to be disruptive, but a more progressive way. And I don’t expect this year, and maybe next year, to occur. So, the probability of occurrence is lower, maybe 20%.

Conclusion

Even though my future prospects in the IP business and the EDA business are quite positive, the impact of those catalysts is not immediate, and it will take years. So, I don’t expect the stock price to get a boost and even catch up with Cadence. The IP business will take a year to regain past success, and royalties will take longer, about two or three more years. Integration with Ansys and agentic software capabilities is also one or two years apart. I would wait, and if the price reaches $478.5 per share, as my target price, I would buy; otherwise, I recommend holding.

data.title: ENFR: Midstream Energy Fund, Good 4.5% Dividend Yield, Not So Good Entry Point
data.description:
ENFR - Overview and Analysis

Index and Portfolio

The Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR) is a midstream energy index ETF, tracking the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index. It is a relatively simple index, including midstream energy corporations and MLPs located in either the U.S. or Canada.

Midstream companies are those focusing on gathering, processing, liquefaction, transportation, and storage of energy products, including crude oil, natural gas, LNG, and others. It is a modified market-cap weighted index, with caps meant to ensure compliance with regulatory standards. ENFR is not liable for corporate taxes, nor do investors have to file a K-1 form.

ENFR provides diversified exposure to midstream energy companies, which isn't saying much - this is a niche subsector of a small equity sector. It invests in 25 different securities, much lower than most equity ETFs. These are as follows:

Although ENFR provides diversified exposure to its investment niche, it does not provide broad-based exposure to the energy sector as a whole, much less to broader U.S. equity markets. ENFR's performance could materially differ from that of its index, and much more so than average. Correlations between the S&P 500 and ENFR are lower than those of most sectors I've seen, including tech (the largest):

Due to the above, I would keep any investment in ENFR small and would make sure to not significantly overweight energy in my portfolio. Smaller positions and some tilts seem better, in my opinion at least.

Revenues, earnings, and cash-flows for blue-chip midstream energy companies are only slightly dependent on energy prices. Blue-chip midstream companies focus on fee-for-service and similar arrangements, with long-term contracts and zero direct commodity price exposure.

As an example, compare EBITDA and cash from operations for ENFR's three largest holdings, with those of Exxon and Chevron during 2020, when energy prices collapsed.

As can be seen above, the three midstream energy companies above saw single-digit declines in their EBITDA and cash from operations during the pandemic. Bad results, but much better than diversified oil majores, which saw declines of around 50% in everything. That is a massive difference, and an incredibly important issue for investors to consider.

On a more negative note, investors don't seem to see things the same way, with most midstream energy companies trading alongside the oil majors when times are tough. Compare their returns during 2020, for instance.

As can be seen above, although there was quite a bit of variation in their performance, midstream saw significant losses during the pandemic, not too disimilar from the oil majors, and far in excess of their financial losses. Although I do not believe said price movement to be all that rational, that is how the market moved, and how the market tends to move during these events. The fact that blue-chip midstream energy companies are less exposed to energy companies matters, as does the fact that the matter does not seem to care all that much about this.

Importantly, not all midstream energy companies behave as above. Some companies, generally the smaller ones, focus on activities or contracts with more significant energy price exposure, seeing significant deterioration in their financials when oil prices decline. I believe that Energy Transfer (ET) is one of these, even though it's a massive company, and even though the GAAP performance was reasonably good during 2020. I do recall ET suffering from significant issues at the time, and the dividends were slashed too:

ENFR invests in over a dozen midstream energy companies, some with significant energy exposure, some with little direct exposure. Overall, I would say the fund has a moderate amount of energy price exposure, although I don't have any figures here.

Dividend Yield

Midstream energy companies tend to distribute excess cash-flows to shareholders as dividends or distributions and generally have good dividend yields. ENFR itself yields 4.6%, a good dividend yield for an equity ETF, quite a bit higher than the equity average, slightly higher than the energy average.

Obviously most fixed-income securities and some niche income producing assets yield more than ENFR, some a lot more, but ENFR is an equity ETF, and could see significant capital gains, much more so than most investments yielding materially more than 4.5%.

ENFR's dividends have seen strong growth since inception, and during most time periods. Lots of exceptions, and distributions do tend to get cut during downturns, recessions, and commodity price crunches.

Dividend growth figures above are accurate, but I don't believe they capture the entirety of the fund's dividend volatility. As an example, dividends decreased by more than 30% in the 2022, something not really noticeable in the figures above.

On a more negative note, ENFR currently trades with a somewhat below-average dividend yield. Dividend yields were much higher during 2020 - 2024, although consistently lower than before the pandemic. Valuations collapsed during the pandemic, energy prices increased during the recovery, explaining these results.

Overall, I'm apprehensive about investing in the fund considering the above. It simply does not look like the best time to buy, buying now means locking-in a comparatively weak yield, weaker than during most of the past five years. Seems risky, and expected income and returns simply do not look all that great.

Valuation

Midstream energy companies have seen their prices and valuations increase these past few years. Ratios are all much higher than they were in early 2024, as per data from this older article of mine:

Valuations for energy companies more broadly are currently at historically above-average levels. Same is probably true of midstream energy companies, but obviously I can't say for certain. Same is definitely true for the broader equity market though.

Overall, this does not look like a good time to buy ENFR. Prices are a bit bad, even though not terrible, dividend yields do not look attractive either. Energy is an incredibly cyclical business, so risks seem even higher accounting for that. At the same time, I don't really see a catalyst for a massive improvement in industry conditions, or in valuations. Economic conditions are such that I could see a (shallow) recession occurring, perhaps a minor increase in inflation from continued tariff pressure. Significantly higher oil prices seem unlikely, I'm not really seeing any potential increase in demand, nor reduction in supply or, more likely, supply chain issues.

On a more positive note, looking at the graph above I'm seeing very good buyback and dividend yields for energy companies. Both are of particular importance to energy companies, as these leverage / are more impactful when valuations are low, which is truer of energy companies than most equities, and because returning significant amounts of cash to shareholders all but precludes the possibility of a massive CAPEX run, making it difficult for oil prices to collapse (smaller price movements are a lot more possible).

Overall, ENFR's performance seems reasonable enough to me, although I'm sure some will find the fund's mediocre long-term returns to be a deal-breaker.

Conclusion

This was a tough call for me. ENFR's valuation does look a bit stretched, but the same is true of the broader equities market. I'm not seeing any positive catalysts, but I'm not really bearish either. The price was better earlier in the month, but not significantly so. Settled on a buy due to general bullishness, and because the negative issues here don't seem to be significant enough as to be a deal-breaker.
